title: Quiz: Stochastic Processes in Finance
description: Test your understanding of Brownian motion, GBM, and Monte Carlo simulation

questions.question: What is the variance of a standard Brownian motion W_t at time t?
questions.explanation: For standard Brownian motion, Var(W_t) = t. The variance grows linearly with time.

questions.question: Why do we use Geometric Brownian Motion instead of regular Brownian motion for stock prices?
questions.explanation: GBM models the logarithm of prices, ensuring prices stay positive. Regular Brownian motion can go negative.

questions.question: In the GBM equation dS = μS dt + σS dW, what does σ represent?
questions.explanation: σ (sigma) represents the volatility - the standard deviation of returns - which multiplies the random Brownian motion component.

questions.question: How does Monte Carlo estimation error scale with the number of samples N?
questions.explanation: Monte Carlo error decreases as O(1/√N). To halve the error, you need 4× more samples.

questions.question: Under Geometric Brownian Motion, stock prices follow a normal distribution.
questions.explanation: False. Under GBM, stock prices follow a log-normal distribution. The logarithm of prices follows a normal distribution.

questions.question: What type of process would you use to model interest rates that tend to return to a long-term average?
questions.explanation: Mean-reverting processes like Ornstein-Uhlenbeck are used for interest rates because they pull back toward a long-term mean.

questions.question: What speedup can quantum amplitude estimation provide over classical Monte Carlo?
questions.explanation: Quantum amplitude estimation provides quadratic speedup, reducing error scaling from O(1/√N) to O(1/N).

questions.question: Which are properties of standard Brownian motion? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Has paths that are everywhere differentiable

questions.explanation: Brownian motion has W_0=0, independent increments, and Gaussian increments. However, paths are continuous but nowhere differentiable (very rough).
